disease.name: Liver Abscess
disease.note: Pus collection within the liver parenchyma. Two main types: pyogenic (bacterial, often from biliary tract or portal vein) and amoebic (Entamoeba histolytica from colonic infection). In East Africa, amoebic liver abscess is common due to endemic amebiasis and poor sanitation. Pyogenic abscesses often follow cholangitis or abdominal infections.
presentingComplaints: Pain in the right upper abdomen
pathophysiologyDetail: Pyogenic liver abscess: bacteria reach liver via (1) biliary tract (ascending cholangitis — most common, 40-60%), (2) portal vein (pylephlebitis from appendicitis, diverticulitis, IBD), (3) hepatic artery (bacteremia from any source), (4) direct extension from adjacent infection, (5) penetrating trauma

Bacteriology of pyogenic abscess: E. coli (30-40%), Klebsiella pneumoniae (20-30%) — especially K. pneumoniae with hypermucoviscous phenotype causing invasive syndrome, Streptococcus spp., Enterococcus, anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium); often polymicrobial (30-50%)

Klebsiella pneumoniae invasive syndrome: hypermucoviscous K1/K2 serotypes → monomicrobial liver abscess → metastatic infection (endophthalmitis, meningitis, necrotizing fasciitis, lung abscess) — common in East Asia but increasingly seen globally; associated with diabetes

Amoebic liver abscess: Entamoeba histolytica exists as cysts (infective form, transmitted fecal-oral) and trophozoites (invasive form); ingested cysts → excyst in small intestine → trophozoites invade colonic mucosa → portal venous drainage to liver → proteolytic enzymes (cysteine proteinases, amebapores) cause tissue lysis

Amoebic abscess content: 'anchovy sauce' — thick, reddish-brown, odorless pus (liquefied liver tissue); it is bacteriologically sterile unless secondarily infected; trophozoites are found in the abscess wall, not the pus (need to scrape the wall of the abscess cavity)

Abscess wall evolution: acute phase (focal suppuration → liquefactive necrosis → cavity formation); chronic phase (>2 weeks) → fibrous capsule formation → thick-walled abscess → may calcify; right lobe affected in 80% (due to streaming of portal venous blood from the right colon)

E. histolytica virulence factors: galactose/N-acetyl-galactosamine-specific lectin (adherence to colonic epithelium), amebapores (pore-forming proteins that lyse host cells), cysteine proteinases (degrade extracellular matrix, cleave IgG and IgA)

Amoebic liver abscess complications: rupture into pleural space (empyema — most common extrahepatic complication, 10-20%), peritoneum (peritonitis), pericardium (tamponade — left lobe abscess), and bronchial tree (bronchobiliary fistula); ameboma (granulomatous mass in colon mimicking carcinoma)

Portal pylephlebitis: septic thrombophlebitis of the portal vein from intra-abdominal infection (appendicitis, diverticulitis, pancreatitis) → septic emboli to the liver → multiple pyogenic abscesses at the periphery of the liver; CT shows thrombus in portal vein with surrounding inflammation

Immunocompromised host: diabetic patients have higher risk of pyogenic abscess (particularly Klebsiella), more complications, and higher mortality; HIV patients (especially with low CD4) have atypical organisms (TB, MAC, Cryptococcus, Nocardia, fungal abscesses) and more severe disease

Tropical pyomyositis-associated abscess: Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia from soft tissue infections can seed the liver via hepatic artery; multiple small abscesses common; requires prolonged anti-staphylococcal therapy

East African context: amoebic liver abscess is endemic due to poor sanitation and contaminated water sources in rural areas; pyogenic abscess commonly follows cholangitis from gallstones (rising in urban areas); HIV-associated liver abscess is an important differential in immunocompromised patients; access to percutaneous drainage is limited at district level

Hydatid cyst (Echinococcus granulosus) vs liver abscess: hydatid is a cystic parasite not a true abscess; daughter cysts, calcified wall, positive serology; rupture causes anaphylaxis; management (PAIR: puncture, aspiration, injection, reaspiration) is different from abscess management

Parasitic co-infection: Schistosoma mansoni (periportal fibrosis) and E. histolytica (amebic liver abscess) co-exist in areas with poor sanitation along Lake Victoria; hepatobiliary schistosomiasis can complicate management of liver abscess by altering liver anatomy and portal hemodynamics

landmarkTrials.trial: Percutaneous Drainage vs Medical Therapy for Pyogenic Liver Abscess (1997, British Journal of Surgery — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 100 patients with pyogenic liver abscess randomized to percutaneous drainage + antibiotics vs antibiotics alone
landmarkTrials.intervention: Percutaneous catheter drainage (8-12 Fr) + 4 weeks IV antibiotics vs medical therapy alone
landmarkTrials.outcome: Drainage group: faster resolution (7 vs 14 days), fewer treatment failures (8% vs 32%), lower mortality (2% vs 12%). Abscesses >5 cm particularly benefited. Abscesses <3 cm can be treated with antibiotics alone.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Percutaneous drainage + antibiotics is standard; small abscesses <3 cm may be treated medically

landmarkTrials.trial: Catheter Drainage vs Needle Aspiration for Liver Abscess (2004, Radiology — Meta-analysis)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 12 studies comparing catheter drainage vs needle aspiration for liver abscess
landmarkTrials.intervention: Percutaneous catheter drainage (pigtail catheter left in situ) vs needle aspiration (single or serial)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Catheter drainage: higher success (92% vs 68%, p<0.01), fewer repeat procedures (8% vs 30%), similar hospital stay. Needle aspiration acceptable for abscesses <5 cm. Catheter preferred for >5 cm.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Catheter drainage preferred for large abscesses; needle aspiration for smaller ones

landmarkTrials.trial: Metronidazole vs Tinidazole for Amoebic Liver Abscess (1988, Clinical Infectious Diseases — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 80 patients with amoebic liver abscess randomized to metronidazole vs tinidazole
landmarkTrials.intervention: Metronidazole 800 mg PO TID × 10 days vs Tinidazole 2 g PO daily × 5 days
landmarkTrials.outcome: Tinidazole: higher clinical cure (95% vs 82%), faster defervescence (3 vs 5 days), fewer side effects (10% vs 25%). Both effective. Tinidazole preferred for shorter course and better tolerability.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Both available in Kenya; metronidazole is cheaper and on essential medicines list

landmarkTrials.trial: Antibiotic Duration for Pyogenic Liver Abscess (2016, Clinical Infectious Diseases — Systematic Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Review of 15 studies on optimal antibiotic duration for pyogenic liver abscess
landmarkTrials.intervention: Short course (<4 weeks total) vs long course (>4 weeks) IV + oral antibiotics
landmarkTrials.outcome: No difference in recurrence or mortality with short vs long course. 2-4 weeks total (IV 1-2 weeks + oral 1-2 weeks) is sufficient if source controlled and drainage adequate. Longer duration if inadequate drainage.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Shorter antibiotic courses reduce cost and drug resistance; practical in Kenya

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic Drainage vs Percutaneous Drainage for Liver Abscess (2014, Surgical Endoscopy — Retrospective)
landmarkTrials.population: 150 patients comparing laparoscopic vs percutaneous drainage of pyogenic liver abscess
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic drainage vs percutaneous catheter drainage
landmarkTrials.outcome: Laparoscopic: higher success (97% vs 82%), shorter drainage time (4 vs 7 days), but longer operative time and general anesthesia requirement. Percutaneous first-line; laparoscopic rescue for failed percutaneous.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Percutaneous first-line; laparoscopic drainage available at select centers for complex cases

landmarkTrials.trial: Luminal Amoebicide after Metronidazole for Amoebic Abscess (1995, The Lancet — RCT)
landmarkTrials.population: 90 patients with amoebic liver abscess after metronidazole course
landmarkTrials.intervention: Paromomycin 25-35 mg/kg/day × 7 days vs placebo after metronidazole
landmarkTrials.outcome: Paromomycin group: 2% fecal cyst carriage at follow-up vs 60% in placebo (p<0.001). Luminal agent eradicates intestinal carriage and prevents reinfection and spread. Essential to complete regimen.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Luminal amoebicide is essential after metronidazole to prevent transmission in Kenyan communities

landmarkTrials.trial: Source Control in Pyogenic Liver Abscess (2012, HPB — Multicenter)
landmarkTrials.population: 435 patients with pyogenic liver abscess — identifying source and controlling it
landmarkTrials.intervention: Investigate and treat underlying source (biliary: ERCP/stent; portal: treat colitis/diverticulitis; cryptogenic: CT colonography)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Source identified in 70%: biliary 50%, portal 15%, cryptogenic 30%. Treating the source reduced recurrence from 25% to 5% at 12 months (p<0.01). CT colonography recommended for cryptogenic abscess.
landmarkTrials.kenyaRelevance: Identifying source is critical in Kenya — ERCP for biliary, CT for cryptic source

evidenceTable.therapy: Percutaneous drainage + ABx vs ABx alone for pyogenic abscess (RCT)

evidenceTable.recommendation: Catheter drainage for >5 cm; aspiration for <5 cm

evidenceTable.therapy: Metronidazole vs Tinidazole for amoebic abscess (RCT)
Short vs long antibiotic duration for pyogenic abscess
evidenceTable.recommendation: 2-4 weeks total if source controlled and drainage adequate

evidenceTable.population: Pyogenic abscess not responding to medical therapy
Pyogenic abscess — source identified and treated

evidenceTable.therapy: Piperacillin-Tazobactam vs Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole for pyogenic abscess
evidenceTable.recommendation: Either regimen acceptable; choose based on local availability
Positive serology + typical imaging = amoebic abscess, treat without aspiration

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical suspicion — febrile patient with RUQ pain, hepatomegaly, weight loss, history of dysentery (amoebic) or biliary disease (pyogenic); subacute course over 5-14 days typical; acute septic presentation suggests pyogenic with cholangitis

Step 2: Abdominal ultrasound — first-line: hypoechoic/anechoic lesion with debris levels, thick irregular wall, posterior acoustic enhancement. Right lobe most common (80%). Differentiate from simple cyst (thin wall, anechoic, no debris), hydatid (daughter cysts, calcification), tumor (solid, vascular)

Step 3: Laboratory — WBC (leukocytosis in pyogenic), CRP (high in both, very high in pyogenic), procalcitonin (high in pyogenic, normal in amoebic), LFTs (elevated ALP/GGT — cholestatic pattern; mild transaminitis), albumin (low in chronic), blood cultures (50% positive in pyogenic)

Step 4: Amoebic serology (IHA or ELISA) — positive in >90% of amoebic liver abscess; negative in early infection (<7 days). Stool microscopy: only 30-50% positive for E. histolytica in amoebic abscess. PCR on pus or stool is most specific

Step 5: CT abdomen with IV contrast — better anatomical detail: hypodense lesion with peripheral enhancement (abscess capsule), cluster sign (multiple small collections aggregating — pyogenic), gas within lesion (gas-forming organisms), extrahepatic extension

Step 6: Aspiration (US/CT-guided) — indications: diagnostic uncertainty, large abscess >5 cm, left lobe abscess, failure to improve after 48-72h of medical therapy, suspected pyogenic where cultures guide antibiotics. Pus: send for Gram stain, culture (aerobic + anaerobic), amoebic trophozoites (wet mount of abscess wall aspirate)

Step 7: Differentiate pyogenic vs amoebic: clinical (dysentery → amoebic; biliary disease → pyogenic), lab (procalcitonin high → pyogenic), aspirate (anchovy sauce → amoebic; foul-smelling pus → pyogenic), culture (positive → pyogenic)
Step 8: Chest X-ray — elevated right hemidiaphragm, right pleural effusion (reactive or empyema), subphrenic air. May show lung abscess if bronchobiliary fistula

Step 9: Identify source — US/MRCP for biliary stones (pyogenic); colonoscopy for colitis/IBD (pyogenic portal source); stool O&P/serology for amoeba. CT colonography for cryptogenic abscess to exclude occult colon malignancy

Step 10: Treatment — amoebic: metronidazole + luminal agent ± drainage. Pyogenic: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics + percutaneous drainage if >3-5 cm. Surgical drainage for complicated cases (rupture, inaccessible location, failed percutaneous)

Kenya adaptation: US widely available; amoebic serology at provincial labs; CT at major centers; percutaneous drainage available at county hospitals; surgical drainage is the fallback when interventional radiology unavailable
managementPearls: For amoebic liver abscess, do NOT aspirate routinely — most (80-90%) resolve with metronidazole alone; aspiration is reserved for large >5 cm, left lobe (pericardial rupture risk), failure to improve after 72h, or diagnostic uncertainty

The anchovy sauce pus of amoebic abscess is sterile — bacteria are not present unless secondary infection has occurred; sending cultures of amoebic abscess pus is unnecessary unless superinfection is suspected

After completing metronidazole (10 days), a LUMINAL AMOEBICIDE (paromomycin 25-35 mg/kg/day × 7 days or diloxanide furoate 500 mg TID × 10 days) must be given to eradicate intestinal cyst carriage — this prevents reinfection and community transmission (NNT 2)

For pyogenic abscess, blood cultures should be drawn before antibiotics but do not delay antibiotics if septic; E. coli and Klebsiella are the most common organisms; anaerobes are present in 30-50% — always cover with metronidazole

Percutaneous catheter drainage: use an 8-12 Fr pigtail catheter; irrigate the cavity with 10-20 mL normal saline daily to prevent clogging; remove when output <10 mL/day for 2 consecutive days and the patient is afebrile — typically 5-10 days

Abscesses that fail to improve after 3-5 days of drainage + appropriate antibiotics should be reassessed with CT: check for (1) undrained loculations, (2) catheter malposition, (3) resistant organisms, (4) incorrect diagnosis (tumor, hydatid), (5) ongoing source (biliary obstruction)

Left lobe liver abscesses are more dangerous than right lobe — they have a higher risk of rupture into the pericardium (pericardial tamponade — mortality 30-50%) and peritoneum; have a lower threshold for drainage and early surgical consultation

In diabetic patients with liver abscess, check for Klebsiella pneumoniae hypermucoviscous syndrome — if the patient has concurrent endophthalmitis (painful vision loss), meningitis, or necrotizing fasciitis, suspect K. pneumoniae invasive syndrome and treat aggressively with carbapenem + consider ocular consultation

Cryptogenic pyogenic liver abscess (no biliary, portal, or arterial source identified) should prompt CT colonography — up to 10-15% of cryptogenic abscesses are associated with occult colorectal cancer, likely due to transient portal bacteremia from mucosal disruption

Follow-up imaging: perform US or CT after 2-4 weeks to confirm resolution; residual cavity (fibrotic wall without pus) may persist for months and does not require further treatment if the patient is well and inflammatory markers are normal

In HIV patients with liver abscess, consider atypical organisms: M. tuberculosis (TB abscess — cold, minimal inflammation, thick wall), MAC, Nocardia, Cryptococcus, and fungal abscesses (Candida, Aspergillus); cultures and special stains are essential
operativeNuances: Percutaneous drainage: use the shortest, safest trajectory avoiding pleura, lung, colon, and gallbladder; subcostal approach preferred over intercostal (higher risk of empyema); US guidance is standard; CT guidance for deeper lesions or those near critical structures

Seldinger technique for catheter drainage: 18G needle → 0.035-inch J-tip guidewire → dilate tract → insert 8-12 Fr pigtail catheter; confirm position with contrast injection; aspirate as much pus as possible; flush with saline and connect to drainage bag

Needle aspiration technique: 18G or 20G spinal needle under US guidance; aspirate as much pus as possible in one session; repeat aspiration if symptoms recur (30% need second aspiration); less effective than catheter drainage for abscesses >5 cm but avoids indwelling catheter

Surgical drainage (laparoscopic): indicated when percutaneous drainage fails, abscess is inaccessible (dome of liver, caudate lobe), multiple large abscesses with septations, ruptured abscess with peritonitis, or concurrent biliary pathology requiring surgery (cholecystectomy)

Laparoscopic technique: 3-port approach (umbilical camera, right subcostal for grasper, right flank for aspirator); identify abscess on the liver surface; aspirate pus; open the cavity and break down loculations; irrigate cavity; place drain into the cavity and bring out through a port site

Open surgical drainage: via a right subcostal (Kocher) incision; indicated for ruptured abscess with generalized peritonitis, failed laparoscopic drainage, or when the abscess is in a location unsafe for laparoscopic approach; thoroughly lavage the peritoneal cavity

In resource-limited settings where percutaneous drainage is not feasible, open surgical drainage remains a life-saving approach; the procedure is well-established, requires minimal equipment, and can be performed at district hospitals by general surgeons

Damage control for ruptured amoebic liver abscess: the patient is often in septic shock; control contamination (drainage and lavage), place drains, close abdomen temporarily (Bogota bag); re-look in 48-72h for definitive washout and closure

Biliary source control at time of drainage: if the abscess is secondary to cholangitis from CBD stones, combined management with ERCP + sphincterotomy + stone extraction is needed simultaneously or staged, depending on the patient's stability

Bronchobiliary fistula management: if biloptysis (coughing up bile) develops, this indicates a fistula between the abscess and the bronchial tree; treatment includes percutaneous drainage of the abscess, ERCP with sphincterotomy ± stent, and antibiotics; surgical repair if fistula does not close

prognosis.amoebicAbscessUncomplicated: >95% cure with metronidazole + luminal agent; mortality <1% with appropriate therapy; abscess resolves completely over 3-6 months radiologically
prognosis.pyogenicAbscessUncomplicated: 85-95% cure with percutaneous drainage + antibiotics; mortality 5-10% (higher in elderly, diabetics, multiple abscesses, delayed treatment)
prognosis.rupturedAbscess: Mortality 15-30% with peritonitis; 30-50% with pericardial involvement; emergency surgical drainage significantly improves outcomes
prognosis.multipleBilateralAbscesses: Mortality 20-40%; more difficult to drain completely; prolonged antibiotics needed; underlying biliary or portal source often severe
prognosticFactors: Abscess type (amoebic has better prognosis than pyogenic)
Size and number — large >10 cm, multiple, and bilateral have worse outcomes
Patient age and comorbidities — elderly, diabetics, immunosuppressed have higher mortality
Complications — rupture, empyema, pericardial extension significantly worsen prognosis
Delay in treatment — each day of delay increases mortality and complication rates
Source control — failure to identify/treat underlying biliary or portal source predicts recurrence
Causative organism — Klebsiella invasive syndrome, resistant organisms, fungal abscess worse prognosis
prognosis.eastAfricanSpecific: Amoebic abscess common in rural Kenya with good prognosis if treated; pyogenic abscess increasing with gallstone disease in urban areas; late presentation and limited drainage availability contribute to higher mortality (10-15% in Kenyan series); HIV co-infection complicates management

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Rupture/multiple/large/sepsis -> SEVERE; small single abscess -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: How long have you had fever? Do you have shaking chills?
Do you have pain in the right upper abdomen?
Have you lost weight or felt very weak recently?
Have you had diarrhea with blood or mucus (dysentery) recently?
Do you have known gallbladder stones or biliary problems?
Have you had any recent abdominal surgery?
Do you drink untreated water from rivers or wells?
Have you been diagnosed with HIV?
Have you traveled recently within East Africa?
Do you eat food from street vendors or unhygienic places?
Have you been treated for amoeba or dysentery before?
Have you had a blood transfusion in the past?
Do you have a cough or chest pain?
Have you noticed yellowing of your eyes?
Have you had any recent dental work or IV drug use?

examination_findings.finding: Right upper quadrant tenderness — marked on palpation
Right lower chest dullness — reactive pleural effusion
Decreased breath sounds right base — splinting from pain
Jaundice — mild, due to biliary obstruction or sepsis
Intercostal tenderness — right side, over liver area
Signs of dehydration — dry mucosa, reduced skin turgor
Pallor from anemia of chronic infection
Cachexia and muscle wasting in chronic abscess
Peritonitis if abscess rupture — generalized guarding, rigidity
Pleural rub or effusion signs on the right
Tachypnea from pleuritic pain or effusion

bedside_scores.name: Pyogenic vs Amoebic Abscess Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Pyogenic score ≥3: likely pyogenic; amoebic score with dysentery: likely amoebic. Serology and aspiration confirm.

riskFactors.note: Most common cause of pyogenic liver abscess
Obstructed CBD leads to ascending infection
E. histolytica reaches liver via portal vein
Increased amoebic exposure in developing regions
Impaired immunity and increased risk of trauma

subtypes.name: Pyogenic (Bacterial) Liver Abscess
subtypes.management: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g 6-hourly or Ceftriaxone 2g daily + Metronidazole 500mg 8-hourly). Percutaneous drainage (US or CT-guided) if >3-5cm. Surgical drainage (laparoscopic or open) if percutaneous fails, inaccessible, or multiple large abscesses. Identify and treat source (cholecystectomy, ERCP).

subtypes.name: Amoebic (Entamoeba histolytica) Liver Abscess
subtypes.management: Metronidazole 750mg IV/PO 8-hourly for 10 days (or Tinidazole 2g daily for 5 days). Followed by luminal amoebicide: Paromomycin 25-35 mg/kg/day in 3 doses for 7 days or Diloxanide furoate 500mg 8-hourly for 10 days. Percutaneous drainage if: large >5cm, left lobe abscess (rupture risk), impending rupture, failure to improve after 72h of medical therapy, or bacterial superinfection.

mimics.whyMimic: Liver mass, weight loss, RUQ pain
Fever, RUQ pain, elevated diaphragm on CXR

bedside.name: Temperature chart
bedside.indication: Fever pattern — high, swinging fevers common

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: Marked leukocytosis with left shift in pyogenic

laboratory.name: LFTs (Bilirubin, ALT, AST, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: Elevated ALP and GGT — cholestatic pattern; mild transaminitis

laboratory.name: Amoebic serology (IHA, ELISA)
laboratory.indication: Positive in >90% of amoebic liver abscess

laboratory.name: Stool microscopy for E. histolytica
laboratory.indication: Positive in only 30-50% of amoebic abscess

laboratory.name: Procalcitonin
laboratory.indication: Elevated in pyogenic, normal in amoebic

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: First-line: hypoechoic/anechoic lesion, debris levels, wall thickness

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: Better characterization, detect smaller abscesses, complications

imaging.name: US/CT-guided aspiration ± drainage
imaging.indication: Diagnostic and therapeutic — send pus for culture, gram stain, amoebae

resuscitation: NBM if considering drainage or if vomiting
IV fluids: Hartmann's or Normal Saline
IV Paracetamol 1g 6-hourly for fever and pain
Blood cultures before antibiotics (do not delay if septic)
antibiotics: Alternative: Ceftriaxone 2g IV daily + Metronidazole 500mg IV 8-hourly
Adjust based on culture and sensitivity (duration 4-6 weeks total, may switch to oral after 2 weeks)
Amoebic: Metronidazole 750mg IV/PO 8-hourly for 10 days + luminal agent
If mixed infection suspected: add broad-spectrum coverage

surgical.indication: Large >5cm, failure of medical therapy, multiple abscesses, left lobe, impending rupture
surgical.percutaneous: US or CT-guided pigtail catheter drainage (8-12 Fr). Irrigate cavity with saline. Leave drain in until output <10 mL/day.
surgical.surgical: Laparoscopic or open drainage for inaccessible location, multiple abscesses, failed percutaneous drainage, ruptured abscess with peritonitis
surgical.marsupialization: Rarely needed — for large chronic abscess with thick wall

nonOperative: Antibiotics alone: for small abscesses (<3cm) without sepsis
Medical therapy: for uncomplicated amoebic abscess — metronidazole + luminal agent
Serial aspiration: alternative to catheter drainage for medium-sized abscesses

general: Continue IV antibiotics based on culture results
Monitor drain output — daily volume, color, culture
Drain removal when output <10 mL/day and afebrile
Repeat imaging to confirm resolution before drain removal
drain_care: Irrigate with 10-20 mL Normal Saline if drain clogs
Drain site care — keep clean, dry
Collect sample for culture weekly if prolonged drainage
discharge_criteria: WBC and CRP trending down significantly
Plan for drain removal as outpatient or at follow-up

pathways.criteria: Size <3 cm, no sepsis, single abscess, no complications
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics alone (broad-spectrum for pyogenic; Metronidazole for amoebic). Monitor with repeat imaging at 2 weeks.

pathways.criteria: Size >5 cm, multiple abscesses, sepsis, left lobe, impending rupture
pathways.treatment: IV antibiotics. US/CT-guided percutaneous drainage. Surgical drainage (laparoscopic or open) if percutaneous fails, inaccessible location, or ruptured abscess.

operative_templates.indication: Large liver abscess (>5cm), multiple abscesses, failure of medical therapy
steps: Patient supine or left lateral position
US or CT scan to identify safest window for drainage
Sterile prep and drape, local anesthesia (1% Lidocaine)
Needle puncture with 18G needle into abscess cavity
Aspirate pus — send for culture, gram stain, amoebic exam
Pass guidewire through needle using Seldinger technique
Confirm position — aspirate remaining pus, flush with saline
Secure drain to skin with suture and sterile dressing

watchFor: sudden drop in output with fever
return of fever and RUQ pain
persistent drainage tract after catheter removal

dischargeCriteria: Afebrile for 48-72 hours off IV antibiotics (or step-down to oral)
WBC and CRP trending down significantly
Follow-up imaging showing abscess resolution or significant reduction
Plan for drain removal confirmed (if applicable)

patient_education.explanation: A liver abscess is a pocket of pus that forms inside your liver due to infection. The two main types are: (1) amoebic — caused by a parasite from contaminated food/water that travels from the gut to the liver, and (2) pyogenic — caused by bacteria that reach the liver from the bile ducts or bloodstream. Treatment includes antibiotics or antiparasitic medication, and sometimes drainage of the pus with a needle or tube.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if: fever returns or worsens, you develop severe abdominal pain, you become short of breath or develop chest pain, you cough up brownish fluid, you feel dizzy or faint, or your drainage tube comes out or stops draining.
patient_education.activity: Rest during treatment. After discharge, gradually increase activity over 1-2 weeks. Avoid heavy lifting for 4 weeks if you had a drain placed. You can resume normal activities when you feel better.
patient_education.diet: Eat a nutritious diet to help your body fight infection. Include plenty of protein (eggs, fish, chicken, beans). Drink clean, boiled, or treated water. Avoid uncooked vegetables and fruits that cannot be peeled. Wash hands thoroughly before eating.
patient_education.medication: Complete the full course of antibiotics or metronidazole as prescribed. Do not stop early. If you had amoebic abscess, you need a second medication (paromomycin or diloxanide) to clear the parasite from your gut even if you feel well. Take all medications as directed.
patient_education.followUp: Return in 2 weeks for clinical review and repeat ultrasound/CT. If you have a drain: weekly follow-up until drain removed. Repeat imaging in 4-6 weeks to confirm complete resolution. Ensure safe water and sanitation at home.

before_discharge: Return-to-work plan based on physical demands

after_discharge.action: Clinical review — drain assessment if applicable, symptom check
Repeat US or CT to assess abscess resolution
Clinical review; LFTs, CRP normalization; luminal agent completion check
Final imaging to confirm complete resolution

when_to_return: Shortness of breath or chest pain
Drain: blockage, falling out, or change in drainage color/odor
Vomiting or inability to keep medications down
